title: Bolstering Security Against Ghost Guns Act
short_title: Bolstering Security Against Ghost Guns Act
plain_summary: This bill tells the Department of Homeland Security to make a plan to deal with ghost guns within one year. Ghost guns are homemade firearms that are very hard for police to track or trace. The government agencies must study these guns, figure out how dangerous they are, and write reports to Congress about problems they cause. They will look at issues like ghost guns being brought across borders and taken into airports where they should not be allowed.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Border Security and Enforcement.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Bolstering Security Against Ghost Guns Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Department of Homeland Security Policy to Bolster Security Against Threats Posed by Ghost Guns
sections.text: This section adds a new requirement to existing homeland security law. Within one year of this law passing, the Department of Homeland Security must create a department wide plan to protect against threats from ghost guns. Ghost guns are privately made firearms that often lack serial numbers and are harder to trace. This plan must help prevent, prepare for, and respond to homeland security threats like terrorism or targeted violence that involve ghost guns. The plan must identify activities across the department related to ghost guns and ways to improve information sharing and teamwork between different parts of the department. It must also identify activities with federal, state, local, tribal, and territorial partners and ways to improve collaboration with these partners. Within one hundred eighty days, the intelligence division must work with border protection, immigration enforcement, and other federal partners to create and share a threat assessment about ghost guns and partially complete gun parts that cross borders. This assessment must pay special attention to ghost guns used by criminal organizations that threaten border security. Within one year, the Secret Service must research and publish public reports with guidance on how to prevent, prepare for, and respond to homeland security threats involving ghost guns. The Transportation Security Administration must report yearly to congressional committees about violations at airport security checkpoints involving firearms, including how many involved ghost guns and other details about trends and prevention efforts. Immigration and Customs Enforcement must prepare an analysis using data from federal partners about security threats and smuggling patterns involving American made firearms, including ghost guns, that are found in Mexico. They must also develop ways to share this information and create measures to assess how well their anti smuggling efforts are working. The intelligence division must create reports every year starting within one hundred eighty days about homeland security threats from ghost guns connected to foreign terrorist organizations or individuals planning terrorism or targeted violence. These reports must assess where these weapons come from, identify different types of threats, and specify threat levels. The reports must be shared with law enforcement at all levels and submitted to congressional committees while protecting classified information. The section includes definitions for key terms like act of terrorism, domestic terrorism, firearm, foreign terrorist organization, ghost gun, targeted violence, and partially complete frame or receiver, all referencing existing federal laws and regulations.
